text: The surgeon assesses a floppy diaphragm with redundant tissue, identifying the tendinous and muscular portions. Using a Cavidian EndoStitch, they place a non-absorbable braided suture with a Teflon pledger, performing horizontal mattress sutures to tighten the muscular fibers above and below the first plication stitch. The pledgers are positioned on either side to prevent tearing due to strain.

The surgeon places the first suture using a simple stitch due to a bent needle, followed by horizontal mattress sutures. The diaphragm is initially high, causing the lung to obstruct the view, but this improves with more sutures. A Maryland Grasper is used for suturing, and intercostal blocks are applied for pain management. Ports are positioned with a ten-millimeter port behind the scapula and a five-millimeter port to the left. The third suture creates a large space under the diaphragm, ensuring no abdominal organs are sutured.

The surgeon uses a knot pusher to tie the fourth horizontal mattress suture in an anteromedial to posterolateral direction across the diaphragm. The sutures create a long, tight line to distribute the load effectively. Occasional bleeding is observed, but it is controlled by pushing down and tying the sutures.

The surgeon demonstrates the placement of horizontal mattress sutures on the diaphragm, showing the overlapping of tissue as the plication lines are formed. The sutures are placed centrally first, followed by lateral or medial ones, ensuring even load distribution to prevent cutting through the tissue. The fifth suture is completed, with the setup for the next suture clearly visible.

The surgeon places two drains at the end of the diaphragm plication procedure. A large 20-inch drain is used for air removal and is planned to be removed the next day, while an 18 French drain is left in place for three to four days to manage pleural effusion.

The surgeon completes the medial plication stitches, resulting in an extremely tight plication line on the diaphragm. The final result shows no further ability to tighten the diaphragm, indicating a successful operation. A twenty-french drain is placed anteriorly up to the apex, and an eighteen-french drain is positioned posteriorly.
